title: Matching values to columns
summary: Learn how to correctly match values to columns when inserting data into a table. Understand why specifying columns in your INSERT statements is safer, and how to handle missing or default values.

sketchBlocks.title: Why match values to columns?
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
When inserting data into a table, it's important to match each value to the correct column. This is especially true if the table structure changes or if not all columns are being filled. For example, in the `inventory_items` table, you can insert a new row by explicitly listing the columns:

```sql
INSERT INTO inventory_items (name, category, price, status, is_test, notes, last_updated)
VALUES ('Desk Organizer', 'Accessories', 13.50, 'active', 0, NULL, '2026-02-10');
```

Here, each value matches the column by position. If you omit the column list, you must provide a value for every column in the table, in the correct order.

sketchBlocks.title: What happens if you skip a column?
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
If you skip a column in your INSERT statement, you must either provide a default value, allow NULL, or explicitly list only the columns you want to fill. For example, the `notes` column in `inventory_items` allows NULL, so you can omit it:

```sql
INSERT INTO inventory_items (name, category, price, status, is_test, last_updated)
VALUES ('Sample Keychain', 'Accessories', 3.00, 'active', 0, '2026-02-11');
```

The `notes` column will be set to NULL for this row.

sketchBlocks.title: Safer inserts with explicit columns
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Always specifying the column names in your INSERT statement makes your code safer and more readable. If the table structure changes (for example, a new column is added), your insert statements are less likely to break or insert data into the wrong columns. For example:

```sql
INSERT INTO inventory_items (name, category, price, status, is_test, notes, last_updated)
VALUES ('Travel Mug', 'Kitchen', 8.99, 'draft', 1, 'Promo item', '2026-02-12');
```

This approach avoids accidental data mismatches.

quizDraft.title: Insert a new inventory item with all columns specified
quizDraft.prompt:
Write an INSERT statement that adds a new row to the `inventory_items` table with these values:
- name: 'Mini Notebook'
- category: 'Accessories'
- price: 5.25
- status: 'active'
- is_test: 0
- notes: 'Back to school'
- last_updated: '2026-02-15'

Make sure to specify all columns except `id` (which is auto-incremented).
quizDraft.hint: List all columns except id in the INSERT statement. Match the order of columns and values.

help.concept: When inserting data, explicitly listing columns ensures each value matches the correct column, and avoids errors if the table structure changes.
help.hint_1: Start with `INSERT INTO inventory_items (name, category, price, status, is_test, notes, last_updated)`.
help.hint_2: Provide the values in the same order as the columns listed, and wrap text values in single quotes.

quizDraft.solutionCode:
INSERT INTO inventory_items (name, category, price, status, is_test, notes, last_updated)
VALUES ('Mini Notebook', 'Accessories', 5.25, 'active', 0, 'Back to school', '2026-02-15');

quizDraft.title: Insert a row with a NULL value
quizDraft.prompt:
Add a new item to `inventory_items` with these values:
- name: 'Sample Pen'
- category: 'Accessories'
- price: 2.00
- status: 'active'
- is_test: 1
- notes: (leave blank)
- last_updated: '2026-02-16'

Insert the row so that the `notes` column is set to NULL.
quizDraft.hint: You can use NULL as a value for the notes column.

help.concept: Columns that allow NULL can be set to NULL explicitly in the VALUES list.
help.hint_1: Include NULL (without quotes) for the notes column in your VALUES.
help.hint_2: Make sure the order of columns and values matches, and use NULL for notes.

quizDraft.solutionCode:
INSERT INTO inventory_items (name, category, price, status, is_test, notes, last_updated)
VALUES ('Sample Pen', 'Accessories', 2.00, 'active', 1, NULL, '2026-02-16');

quizDraft.title: Insert a row using default values
quizDraft.prompt:
Insert a new inventory item with these values:
- name: 'Trial Eraser'
- category: 'Accessories'
- price: 1.50
- last_updated: '2026-02-17'

Let the `status` and `is_test` columns use their default values, and set `notes` to NULL. Only specify the columns you are providing values for.
quizDraft.hint: List only the columns you want to provide values for in the INSERT statement.

help.concept: If a column has a default value, you can omit it from the column list and VALUES; the default will be used.
help.hint_1: List name, category, price, notes, and last_updated in the column list.
help.hint_2: Omit status and is_test from the column list and values; set notes to NULL.

quizDraft.solutionCode:
INSERT INTO inventory_items (name, category, price, notes, last_updated)
VALUES ('Trial Eraser', 'Accessories', 1.50, NULL, '2026-02-17');

quizDraft.title: Which statements about matching values to columns are true?
quizDraft.prompt: Select all statements that are true about matching values to columns in INSERT statements.
quizDraft.hint: Think about safety, readability, and what happens if you omit columns.

help.concept: Explicitly listing columns in INSERT statements helps prevent errors and makes your code more robust.
help.hint_1: Consider what happens if the table structure changes or if you omit a column.
help.hint_2: Remember that default values and NULLs can be used for omitted columns, but only if allowed.

options: a. Listing columns in the INSERT statement makes your code safer.
b. If you omit a column with a default value, the default will be used.
c. You must always provide a value for every column, even if it has a default.
d. Omitting the column list can cause data to be inserted into the wrong columns if the table changes.
e. You cannot insert NULL into any column.

quizDraft.title: Fill in the blank: safer INSERT statements
quizDraft.prompt: Complete the sentence: To make your INSERT statements safer and less error-prone, always ______ the columns you are inserting values into.
quizDraft.hint: Think about what you do with the column names in the INSERT statement.

help.concept: Explicitly naming columns in your INSERT statement reduces errors and improves clarity.
help.hint_1: Do you write out the column names or leave them out?
help.hint_2: The answer is a verb describing what you do with the columns in the statement.

quizDraft.template: To make your INSERT statements safer and less error-prone, always ______ the columns you are inserting values into.
